bio.title: Brig. Gen.
bio.photo_notes: Portrait in uniform with G.A.R. medal, Co. C, 39th Massachusetts. Medford Historical Society / Digital Commonwealth.

burial.plot_notes: Oak Grove Cemetery, Medford, Middlesex County MA (per Findagrave).

military_service.notes: Enlisted Aug 10, 1862 at age 16 as 'S. Painter, Medford' in Co. C, 39th Mass Infantry (per Regimental History p. 378). Promoted Corporal May 29, 1864; Sergeant (Color Bearer) March 2, 1865. Mustered out Jun 22, 1865 at Philadelphia PA. Wounded during the war.

Commanded the 5th Massachusetts Infantry during the Spanish-American War. Earlier service in 5th MVM: enlisted Co. E Nov 12, 1859 (pre-Civil War militia). Through successive promotions became Colonel Aug 6, 1897. Promoted Brigadier General Feb 23, 1908.

career.notes: Learned the painters trade in early life.
Worked as a cigar maker after the Civil War, around the time of his 1869 marriage.
12 years on Medford Police Force; served as Chief during the 1880s.

Appointed to MA State Police 1887; rose to Chief in 1897 (per architectural history doc; per Regimental History 'Chief since 1908'). 'In his long connection with the state police Gen. Whitney was active in many prominent murder mysteries and strike disturbances.' Retired 1913 Medford.

sources.short: Regimental History of the 39th Massachusetts Infantry, p. 378
Medford Historical Commission, Magoun-Whitney House report (2017/2021)
S. C. Lawrence Post 66 (Medford GAR), member listing 1898

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War flag bearer; later Brigadier General

narrative_blocks.header: From teenage flag bearer to brigadier general
narrative_blocks.body: Jophanus Whitney enlisted at sixteen in the 39th Massachusetts and carried the colors through the Civil War, rising to color sergeant and taking a wound along the way. Decades later he commanded a regiment in the Spanish-American War and rose to chief of the Massachusetts State Police, made brigadier general in 1908.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped subdue a State House gunman
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1907 a deranged man drew a revolver inside the Massachusetts governor's office and shot a powerful labor leader in the temple; Whitney, the state police chief, was among the men who wrestled him to the floor. Only afterward did Whitney recognize the gunman as a soldier he had served with in the Spanish-American War, court-martialed years before for delusional threats.

narrative_blocks.header: A cigar maker who rose to police chief
narrative_blocks.body: Whitney had worked as a painter and a cigar maker before joining the Medford police, where he became chief in the 1880s, then rose to head the Massachusetts State Police. In that long career he figured in many of the era's prominent murder mysteries and strike disturbances.

narrative_blocks.header: He was sued for exhuming a murder victim
narrative_blocks.body: Whitney's police work once drew a lawsuit when a widow sued him and other officers for disinterring her husband's body. The exhumation had been ordered to recover the bullet that killed the man, as evidence in a murder case.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Bought this lot with his father-in-law in 1868; the family home for decades.
